addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Find where vim mappings are defined

content.text:
To find where a mapping is defined in Vim, use the **`:verbose`** command:

```vim
:verbose map <your-mapping>
```

For example:
```vim
:verbose map <leader>w
```

This will show you what the mapping does AND the file path and line number where it's defined.

**Other useful commands:**

- `:map <mapping>` — shows what a mapping does (without location)
- `:verbose nmap <mapping>` — search in normal mode only (use `imap`, `vmap`, `cmap` for other modes)
- `:scriptnames` — lists all sourced script files in order
- `:set runtimepath?` — shows where Vim looks for config files

Once you know the file, you can jump to it directly in Vim with `:edit <filename>` or search for the mapping pattern using `/` to find the exact line.

content: You asked how to find where Vim mappings are defined. I explained using `:verbose map` and related commands to locate them by file and line number. Ready for your next question about Vim. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: how to test if one identifier is a valid coc doHover target?

content.text:
To test if an identifier is a valid `doHover` target in CoC, you can:

**1. Manual test (interactive):**
```vim
:call CocActionAsync('doHover')
```

Position your cursor on the identifier and run this command. If hover info appears, it's a valid target.

**2. Check if hover info exists (in a script):**
```vim
let result = CocAction('doHover')
if !empty(result)
  echo "Hover info available"
else
  echo "No hover info"
endif
```

**3. Check LSP server capabilities:**

CoC can only provide hover if the language server supports it. Check what your server provides:
```vim
:CocInfo
```

Look for the language server and check if `hover` is in its capabilities.

**4. Debug hover failures:**

If hover isn't working:
- Check `:CocList diagnostics` to see if there are language server errors
- Check `:CocCommand workspace.showOutput` to see LSP output
- Verify the identifier is actually recognized by the LSP (e.g., it's in scope, has valid syntax)
- Some servers only provide hover for certain symbol types (functions, classes, etc.), not variables or arbitrary identifiers

**Key point:** Not all identifiers will have hover information—it depends on what the language server can resolve. Built-in types and well-defined symbols are more likely to have hover info than unknown or generic identifiers.

message.content: no way to check without generate a popup?

content.text:
Unfortunately, CoC doesn't expose a public API to silently check hover availability without triggering the UI.

However, you have a few workarounds:

**1. Suppress the popup (closest option):**
```vim
silent call CocActionAsync('doHover')
" Then immediately close it if needed
if getcmdline() != ''
  normal! <Esc>
endif
```

The `silent` keyword suppresses messages, but the popup may still briefly appear.

**2. Check LSP capabilities directly (won't tell you about *this specific* identifier, but tells you if the server supports hover):**
```vim
" Check if current buffer's LSP server supports hover
let server = CocAction('getCurrentServer')
if server && get(server, 'serverCapabilities', {}).hoverProvider
  echo "Server supports hover"
endif
```

**3. Use the low-level RPC (might not give you what you need):**
```vim
let result = coc#rpc#request('doHover', [])
" But this also triggers the UI
```

**Reality:** CoC's `doHover` is fundamentally a UI command—it's designed to show the popup. There's no built-in silent mode to just check if data exists without the side effect.

If you need to check hover programmatically without UI, you'd likely need to:
- Call the LSP `textDocument/hover` request directly (not exposed in CoC's public API)
- Or write a custom CoC extension using the Node.js side

What's your use case? There might be a better approach depending on what you're trying to do.

aiTitle: Find where vim mappings are defined

content.text:
To trace what's providing the hover data, use:

**1. Find which LSP server is active on the current buffer:**
```vim
:CocList services
```
Shows all active language servers and their attached filetypes.

**2. See raw LSP traffic (including hover requests/responses):**
```vim
:CocCommand workspace.showOutput
```
Then trigger a hover — you'll see the raw `textDocument/hover` request and response in the output log, including which server responded.

**3. Check which server is attached to current file:**
```vim
:echo CocAction('getCurrentServer')
" or
:CocInfo
```

**4. See the actual hover response data:**

In the output log, look for entries like:
```
[LSP] <- textDocument/hover
{"contents": {"kind": "markdown", "value": "..."}}
```

The server name will be labeled in the log (e.g., `[tsserver]`, `[pylsp]`, `[clangd]`).

**Shortest path:** `:CocCommand workspace.showOutput`, then move your cursor to the identifier and run `:call CocActionAsync('doHover')`. The log will show you exactly which server responded and what data it returned — or if it returned `null` (meaning no hover data for that identifier).

message.content: no, I mean, use the lsp directly to see if there's valid return

content.text:
You can call the LSP `textDocument/hover` request directly via CoC's RPC:

```vim
let params = {
  \ 'textDocument': {'uri': 'file://' . expand('%:p')},
  \ 'position': {'line': line('.') - 1, 'character': col('.') - 1}
  \ }
let result = CocAction('runCommand', 'workspace.executeCommand', 'textDocument/hover', params)
```

Or more directly using CoC's internal request:

```vim
let result = coc#rpc#request('doHover', [])
echo result
```

But the cleanest way — use `coc#util#api` if available, or tap into the LSP client directly:

```vim
call coc#client#request('languageserver', 'textDocument/hover', params, {err, result -> execute('echo string(result)')})
```

**Honestly the most reliable way:**

```vim
:CocCommand workspace.showOutput
```

Then in another split, move cursor to identifier and:
```vim
call CocRequest('languageServerId', 'textDocument/hover', {
  \ 'textDocument': {'uri': coc#util#get_current_buf()},
  \ 'position': coc#util#cursor()
  \ })
```

Check `:h CocRequest` — it sends a raw LSP request and returns the result without showing UI, which is exactly what you want.

content: You're trying to silently check if an identifier has valid LSP hover data without triggering CoC's popup. Next step: try `:h CocRequest` to send a raw textDocument/hover request and inspect the return value directly. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content:
I added these for esp project, but are any of these going to affect any other use cases?
    "clangd.arguments": [
      "--background-index",
      "--clang-tidy",
      "--header-insertion=iwyu",
      "--limit-results=0",
      "--all-scopes-completion",
      "--limit-references=0",
      "--background-index-priority=background",
      "--enable-config"
    ]

explain them

content.text:
These are global clangd settings, so yes, they affect **all C/C++ files**. Here's what each does:

| Argument | Effect | Cross-project concern? |
|---|---|---|
| `--background-index` | Indexes the whole project in background for faster completion/references | Low — useful everywhere, just uses CPU/disk |
| `--clang-tidy` | Runs clang-tidy linting as you type | **Medium** — needs a `.clang-tidy` config, may produce noisy warnings in projects without one |
| `--header-insertion=iwyu` | Auto-inserts headers based on "Include What You Use" | **High** — can insert wrong/unexpected headers in non-ESP projects |
| `--limit-results=0` | No limit on completion results | Low — just more results, slightly heavier |
| `--all-scopes-completion` | Completes symbols from all scopes, not just current | Low — generally useful, slightly more noise |
| `--limit-references=0` | No limit on "find references" results | Low — just returns more results |
| `--background-index-priority=background` | Keeps indexing at low CPU priority | Low — actually polite to your system |
| `--enable-config` | Reads `.clangd` config file per-project | **Recommended** — lets you override settings per-project to cancel out the ones above |

**Key takeaway:** `--enable-config` is your safety valve. Add a `.clangd` file in non-ESP projects to override the aggressive ones:

```yaml
# .clangd in a non-ESP project
CompileFlags:
  Add: []
Diagnostics:
  ClangTidy:
    Remove: '*'
```

The most likely to cause problems elsewhere are `--clang-tidy` and `--header-insertion=iwyu`.

message.content:
and they aren't really that useful on esp either huh
I can just remove them?

content.text:
Yeah, for ESP-IDF projects specifically:

- `--clang-tidy` — ESP-IDF has its own coding style, clang-tidy rules often don't apply well and get noisy
- `--header-insertion=iwyu` — ESP-IDF headers have complex include chains, IWYU often inserts wrong ones
- `--limit-results=0` / `--limit-references=0` — ESP-IDF is huge, removing limits can make clangd slow/unresponsive

The ones worth keeping:
- `--background-index` — genuinely useful for navigating ESP-IDF's large codebase
- `--background-index-priority=background` — keeps it from hogging CPU
- `--enable-config` — useful so you can add a per-project `.clangd` file when needed

So a leaner version:
```json
"clangd.arguments": [
  "--background-index",
  "--background-index-priority=background",
  "--enable-config"
]
```

That's a reasonable baseline for any C/C++ project without the risky ones.

message.content: I think I'll keep limit removal cuz I saw somewhere that using clangd on esp generated compile_commands can often be faulty with limit, is that true?

content.text:
I'm not confident that's accurate — I haven't seen documentation or known issues specifically linking `--limit-results=0` / `--limit-references=0` to faulty `compile_commands.json` behavior on ESP-IDF.

Those flags just control **how many results are returned** in completion/references lists — they don't affect how clangd parses or processes the compile commands.

If you saw it somewhere specific, it's worth checking that source again. It's possible the advice was:
- About a different flag (e.g., something affecting how clangd handles compile command parsing)
- Specific to a particular ESP-IDF or clangd version
- Just cargo-culted config with no real basis

I'd say **don't keep them based on that reasoning** — keeping `--limit-results=0` on a large codebase like ESP-IDF can actually make completion sluggish by returning thousands of results. The default limits exist for performance reasons.

message.content:
Q:
Using Arch Linux, Neovim/vscode

New to esp stuff so bear with me. I figured I'd do a cli install of idf tools instead of a gui, to maybe understand what is going on.

I installed it via the Arch AUR, and it placed it inside /opt/esp-idf/. Then in there, I ran \[install.sh\]([http://install.sh](http://install.sh)) to put the files into \\\~/.espressif.

When I launch neovim on a sample project, the header files say they can't be found, specifically:

\`'sys/features.h' file not found \[pp\_file\_not\_found\]\`

So given, this, how can I configure neovim to work with esp32 projects, and give me autocomplete as well as lsp stuff. I couldn't find much information online, as most were using the Arduino IDE/VSCode with extensions. Is this even possible?

I noticed something, if this can aid in pointing me into the right direction. when I write \`which clangd\` inside of a idf activated terminal, it points to the esp-clangd inside of \~/.espressif. When I write that inside of a terminal that is not activated with idf, then it points to /usr/sbin...

Now I'm even more confused, I had thought that the bundled clangd that it points to would have all the header files accounted for when I am using the LSP, so that it doesn't give me false errors/warnings.

Another thing I noticed, I can replicate the same thing on VSCode by using the clangd extension instead of the microsoft c++/c extension pack. The clangd one shows the false errors like neovim and the microsoft extension one doesn't.

Really don't know what to do, I would have thought that running \`idf.py\` \`build\` would create the proper compile\\\_commands.json for the clangd LSP to properly work.



A:
If these are the only errors and it doesn't stop because of exceeding the error limit, that's really good enough. I am in a similar situation, but with plain clangd and a different editor. All of the include paths and necessary stuff is in the compile commands, and once the editor runs clangd it should be able to figure it out.

Run clangd with the args --limit-results=0 --all-scopes-completion --limit-references=0 --background-index --background-index-priority=background --enable-config for more thorough suggestions, then you can try silencing unimportant errors with ~/.config/clangd/config.yaml:

CompileFlags:
    Add:
        [
            -ferror-limit=99999,
            # -I,
            # /usr/lib/modules/6.18.2-arch2-1/build/include/,
            -Wno-implicit-function-declaration,
        ]
    # Remove:
    #     - -mlongcalls
    #     - -mdisable-hardware-atomics
    #     - -mno-target-align
    #     - -fno-shrink-wrap
    #     - -fstrict-volatile-bitfields
    #     - -fno-tree-switch-conversion
    #     - -fzero-init-padding-bits=all
    #     - -fno-malloc-dce
Diagnostics:
    Suppress:
        #         - pp_file_not_found
        - unused-includes
In my case there are a few annoying errors like these, but I can still get perfect language server functionality. I think these probably have to do with IDF being GCC and are deep enough in the code not to really matter.

Are there any fatal ones or only these odd warnings? Try the LSP functionality and checking clangd's output/logs, it might work to some extent anyway or reveal why it doesn't

aiTitle: Find where vim mappings are defined

content.text:
So the reasoning behind `--limit-results=0` and `--limit-references=0` in that answer has nothing to do with `compile_commands.json` being faulty — it's just "more thorough suggestions." The person recommending it is in a similar situation and just threw those flags in for completeness.

There's no technical justification there for keeping them specifically for ESP. My earlier assessment stands — they're optional quality-of-life flags with a performance tradeoff on large codebases. Keep them or drop them based on whether you actually want unlimited results, not because of any ESP-specific issue.
